title: Adhesive types for bookbinding
subtitle: PVA, wheat-paste, rice paste, and methyl cellulose: when to use each and why
excerpt: The four main bookbinding adhesives behave differently in terms of reversibility, working time, and final strength. Knowing which to use at each stage of a binding determines how well it lasts and how easily a future conservator can take it apart.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on adhesives and pasting. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Cockerell covers paste and glue; the modern comparative between PVA and wheat-paste draws on his observations about open time and flexibility.

glossaryTerms.definition: The period after applying adhesive during which the two surfaces can still be repositioned. Short open time requires working quickly; long open time allows adjustment.
The property of an adhesive that allows it to be softened and removed from a bonded joint, typically with water or a solvent, without damaging the substrate.

Polyvinyl acetate, a synthetic adhesive that dries flexible and transparent. The standard bookbinding adhesive for spine work, hinging, and casing in. Acid-free formulations are used for archival work.

attrs.heading: Safety: bookbinding adhesives
attrs.body: PVA and cooked wheat-paste are safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Rubber cement and spray adhesives need ventilation and are best used near an open window. Never use heat to remove book glues: heating them releases acrid fumes.

content.text: A bookbinding adhesive does three things: it bonds, it allows controlled working time, and it ages predictably. A bond that is too rigid cracks the spine when the book opens. A bond with no
reversibility

means a conservator must cut the binding apart to repair it. A bond that yellows or embrittles with age weakens over the lifetime of the book. Different stages of a binding require different adhesives.
PVA, acid-free
Acid-free
PVA
is the workhorse for spine work, lining, and casing in. It bonds firmly, remains flexible when dry, and does not yellow. Its
open time
is short: roughly three to five minutes in cool conditions, less in a warm room. Once it tacks, repositioning distorts the surfaces.

Use acid-free PVA for: spine lining with book mull or super cloth; gluing the spine before rounding and backing; the first coat of cover cloth or paper to a board; and attaching headbands. Do not use it for pastedowns if reversibility matters; wheat-paste is better there.
Wheat-paste (cooked)

Cooked wheat-paste (flour and water, stirred over heat until thick) has a long open time, typically twenty minutes or more. It bonds less aggressively than PVA; two surfaces joined with paste can be separated by dampening with a wet sponge. This reversibility makes it the adhesive of choice for pastedowns, endpapers, and any covering that a conservator might one day need to remove cleanly.

Wheat-paste causes more board warp than PVA because it is wetter. Counter this by applying paste thinly and pressing the board immediately under weights. Paste that is too thick applies unevenly and dries in ridges; thin paste brushes out smoothly.
Use wheat-paste for: pastedowns; endpaper attachment; applying cover paper to boards in a case binding where reversibility is valued. Avoid for spine lining, where PVA's bond strength is needed.
Japanese rice paste

Japanese rice paste (nori) is finer and more adhesive than wheat-paste for its weight. It is the traditional adhesive for washi mending and is used wherever a thin, transparent, reversible bond is needed. Conservators use it for attaching Japanese tissue reinforcements to torn pages and for reattaching detached boards on historic bindings.
For home bookbinding, rice paste is most useful for attaching washi kozo endpapers and for fine mending work. It is less useful for the structural stages of a binding, where PVA's strength is needed.
Paste-PVA mixtures

Mixing acid-free PVA and wheat-paste in roughly equal parts gives an adhesive with the longer open time of paste and closer to the bond strength of PVA. This mixture is widely used for covering boards: it gives time to position the paper or cloth without tacking too fast, but the final bond is stronger than paste alone. Experiment with a 60:40 PVA-to-paste ratio in warm conditions and a 40:60 ratio in cool conditions to extend the open time further.
What not to use

Hot-melt glue is too rigid and cannot be reversed without damaging the paper. Standard school PVA (not acid-free) contains plasticisers that migrate over time and yellow. Rubber cement is not suitable for archival work: it dries brittle, is not reversible without solvents, and off-gasses in storage. Spray adhesive is useful for temporary assemblies only.
